_source: Composed from Sierra's public Agent SDK documentation at sierra.ai/product/develop-your-agent. Sierra does not publish a full tool schema reference publicly — actual schemas are accessible only to customers under NDA. This file documents the patterns Sierra explicitly claims to enforce: skill composition, deterministic API interactions, policy-bound execution.
_sierra_claims_about_tool_invocation: When the agent accesses your systems of record, those interactions are deterministic and controlled — Sierra source: sierra.ai/product/trust-and-reliability
Sensitive payment data flows through dedicated PCI-certified infrastructure and never touches Sierra's core platform, LLMs, or persistent storage
Built-in filters and monitors for topics and keywords that are off-limits based on your company policies
Validator agents review outputs against policy rules before confirming completion — Sierra source: constellation-of-models post

skills.name: lookup_subscription
skills.description: Look up a customer's current subscription state.
skills._note: Read-only skills do not require supervisor review in Sierra's documented architecture.

skills.name: update_subscription
skills.description: Modify a customer's subscription (upgrade, downgrade, pause).
skills._note: Write skills route through a supervisor agent that validates the action against policy before commit. Sierra documents this as 'deterministic and controlled' interactions.

skills.name: process_refund
skills.description: Issue a refund against a transaction.
skills.infrastructure_isolation: Routes through dedicated PCI-certified infrastructure per Sierra's published security model. Refund data never enters Sierra's core platform or LLM context.
skills._note: Sierra's strongest public claim: payment-sensitive operations are architecturally isolated from the LLM.

skills.name: escalate_to_human
skills.description: Live Assist hand-off to a human agent with context.
skills._note: Sierra explicitly retains human escalation as a first-class capability — 'if a member wants to speak to a human, that's always an option.'

validation_model.where: Validator agents in the constellation, separate from the executor agent.
validation_model.what: Outputs reviewed against policy rules before confirming completion.
validation_model._note: Sierra publishes the WHAT (validator agents enforce policy) but not the HOW (no public code, no public rule language). Procurement should request methodology details under NDA.

deterministic_api_layer.description: Sierra's Agent SDK ensures 'deterministic API Interactions' — meaning the LLM does not write raw API calls. Skills are pre-defined functions with typed inputs; the LLM selects which skill and with what arguments; the SDK enforces the call shape.
deterministic_api_layer.buyer_implication: Significantly reduces the risk of LLM hallucinating an API call or an unsafe parameter. Buyer's procurement team should still ask: what happens if the LLM passes a wrong argument that IS schema-valid?

dry_run_mode.mechanism: Simulations — Sierra provides simulation tooling that verifies agent behavior across scenarios before promotion. From sierra.ai/product/develop-your-agent: 'verify your agent performs as expected across a wide range of scenarios and avoid regressions.'
dry_run_mode.scope: Pre-deployment / regression testing. Whether per-execution dry-run for high-risk live actions is supported is not publicly documented.

approval_gate.mechanism: Supervisors enforce guardrails, policies, and quality checks. The exact human-in-the-loop wiring (which actions require approval, who can approve, latency) is not in public material.

_buyer_followups: Request the actual Agent SDK reference under NDA — what does a skill definition look like in code?
Ask for the validator-agent policy DSL or rules language used.
Confirm whether per-execution dry-run is available for high-risk actions (or only batch-mode simulations).
Confirm what happens when a skill is invoked with a schema-valid but semantically wrong argument (e.g. valid customer_id, wrong customer).
